query: is collagen the most abundant protein in humans

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is the most abundant protein in the human body. Therefore, it can be divided into many types. The most common types of collagen are types I through V each serving different functions.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins; therefore, it is no surprise that collagen is comprised of amino [4] acids. The primary amino acid sequence of collagen is glycine-proline-X or glycine-X-hydroxyproline [5] X can be any of the other 17 amino acids, and every third amino acid is glycine.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The synthesis of collagen begins with DNA translation of a polypeptide chain in the rough endoplasmic reticulum of intracellular space (See Figure. Review of Collagen Synthesis). This polypeptide chain is called preprocollagen, composed of alpha chains with repeating amino acid glycine-X-Y sequences (X and Y are proline or lysine). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is protein molecules made up of amino acids. It provides structural support to the extracellular space of connective tissues. Due to its rigidity and resistance to stretching, it is the perfect matrix for skin, tendons, bones, and ligaments.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen can be further divided into several groups depending on the type of structures they form. There are 28 various types of collagen that have been discovered, but by far, the most common are types I through IV, with type I comprising over 90% of the collagen in the human body. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The process of collagen synthesis occurs mainly in the cells of fibroblasts which are specialized cells with the main function of synthesizing collagen and stroma. Collagen synthesis occurs both intracellularly and extracellularly. Although different types of collagen may undergo different post-translational modifications, the basic outline for collagen synthesis is listed below.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is composed of 3 chains. The chains are wound together to form a triple helix. Since glycine is the smallest of all the amino acids, it allows the chain to form a tight configuration, and and it can withstand stress.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Procollagen then shuttles to the extracellular space through exocytosis. Once in the extracellular space, it is cleaved at the C-terminus and N-terminus, forming what is now called tropocollagen. As a result of the cleavage, tropocollagen is insoluble in water.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Scurvy
retrieved_docs.content: This is a family of genetic disorders that affect the bones making them weak and easily breakable. Inheritance is autosomal dominant, and most cases are due to mutations in the COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes. There are 8 types, each with differing degrees of severity with type 1 the mildest and type II the most severe. [7] Gene mutations affect procollagen formation in which the small glycine amino acid is substituted for bulkier amino acids which alter the collagen triple helix structure.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Next, the hydroxylated lysine of the alpha chain undergoes glycosylation with the addition of a carbohydrate. After glycosylation, the formation of disulfide bonds and hydrogen bonds between 3 different alpha chains forms a triple helix. The resultant structure is now procollagen. Defective triple helix formation leads to osteogenesis imperfecta and impaired bone matrix synthesis. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Next, tropocollagen is reinforced with many adjacent tropocollagen molecules by covalent cross-linking of hydroxylated lysine residues, resulting in collagen fibrils. The cross-linking of hydroxylysine residues is made possible through a copper-dependent lysyl oxidase enzyme. Finally, several collagen fibrils accumulate to form a thick bundle; the end product is collagen fibers. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: As with any biochemical pathway, there are numerous steps that are carefully executed and are tightly regulate and controlled. However, with many steps in processing collagen, there can be genetic mutations which can lead to errors in assembly, posttranslational modification or nutritional deficiencies which can affect enzymatic function. Some examples include:

retrieved_docs.title: Collagen Cross Linking for Keratoconus -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cornea comprises distinct, densely placed, multi-scale structures with fibers of distinct patter arranged in various layers. Each layer is composed of distinct components having variations in collagen density, elastin, fibronectin, laminin, and proteoglycan. [6] These components give rise to different mechanical strengths due to various mechanical properties. The cornea is composed of 6 layers which are 550 um thick. A healthy cornea can transmit approximately 80% of light in the visible spectrum. [7] The cornea is densely packed with proteins called collagen. The collagen in the cornea resides for approximately 2-3 years. The corneal transparency is maintained by the regular packing of proteins with a proteoglycan-rich matrix, allowing the light to pass unimpeded. [8] Collagen crosslinking help in the formation of bonds between the polymeric chains of proteins. In biological tissue like cornea, these bonds strengthen the tissue, making them more resistant to mechanical degradation and deformation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Osteogenesis imperfecta which is an autosomal dominant disorder of type 1 collagen, which can present with a spectrum of findings from mild to lethal. Ehlers-Danlos syndrome which is also an inherited collagen disorder with at least 6 different subtypes with different mutations of different collagen types. Lastly, Vitamin C deficiency is a nutritional deficiency which leads to altered hydroxylase enzyme function which requires Vitamin C as a cofactor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Kolagen serves as the parent classification for connective tissue proteins, indicating that connective tissue proteins are members of the kolagen family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2710
retrieved_docs.content: Connective tissue protein is linked to both kyselina hyaluronová and kolagen, indicating that it is considered a subclass or component of these two major extracellular‑matrix substances. The ReFit bone substitute is mapped to kyselina hyaluronová and also to kolagen, showing that the product incorporates or is designed to emulate these key connective‑tissue constituents. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of hyaluronic acid (kyselina hyaluronová) and collagen (kolagen) as foundational elements of connective tissue proteins and as integral components in the formulation of bone‑substitute materials, which can enhance biocompatibility and support bone regeneration in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1463
retrieved_docs.content: Hyaluronic acid (kyselina hyaluronová) and collagen (kolagen) are highlighted as primary components of connective tissue ultrastructure, and both are listed as allowed qualifiers for describing ultrastructural features. Protein hydrolysates (proteinové hydrolyzáty) are similarly recognized as an allowed qualifier for ultrastructure, emphasizing their relevance in detailed tissue morphology. Connective tissue proteins are conceptually linked to both hyaluronic acid and collagen, reflecting their shared role in the extracellular matrix and structural integrity of connective tissues. The ReFit bone substitute is specifically mapped to hyaluronic acid and collagen, indicating that this biomaterial incorporates these two key extracellular matrix constituents to mimic natural bone tissue architecture. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of hyaluronic acid, collagen, and protein hydrolysates in defining tissue ultrastructure and in the design of biomimetic bone substitutes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1296
retrieved_docs.content: Connective tissue protein is described as having both kolagen (collagen) and kyselina hyaluronová (hyaluronic acid) as parent components, indicating that these macromolecules contribute to its structure or classification. In this representation, kyselina hyaluronová is further placed under the broader category of proteiny (proteins), reflecting its inclusion within protein‑related pathways. The ReFit bone substitute is mapped to both kyselina hyaluronová and kolagen, suggesting that the product incorporates these biomaterials to emulate the natural extracellular matrix of bone. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected roles of kolagen, kyselina hyaluronová, and the protein family in connective tissue composition and in the design of bone‑substituting materials.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 910
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a network in which the metabolic domain (metabolismus) is qualified by a variety of biological substances—including amylases, blood‑coagulation factors (faktory krevní koagulace), karaya gum (guma karaya), immunostimulants, bone morphogenetic proteins (kostní morfogenetické proteiny), and hyaluronic acid (kyselina hyaluronová). Metabolismus is further linked to the therapeutic agents omalizumab and thyroglobulin, indicating metabolic considerations for these drugs. Immunostimulants are classified as a type of hyaluronic acid, and the compound acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin is identified as an immunostimulant. Hyaluronic acid and collagen (kolagen) are each characterized by ultrastructural (ultrastruktura) attributes, a property also ascribed to karaya gum, amylases, thyroglobulin, and blood‑coagulation factors. Connective‑tissue protein is defined as having hyaluronic acid and collagen as parent components, reflecting its composition from these extracellular‑matrix molecules. The ReFit bone substitute is mapped to both hyaluronic acid and collagen, indicating incorporation of these substances in the biomaterial. Bone morphogenetic proteins are highlighted as principal regulators of bone tissue ultrastructure, and this relationship is recognized as an allowed qualifier. The therapeutic class “drugs affecting bone structure and mineralization” is closely associated with bone morphogenetic proteins, underscoring the targeting of BMP pathways in treatments for bone remodeling disorders. Omalizumab, while primarily an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody, is also linked to ultrastructure, suggesting potential effects on tissue architecture. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate how metabolic qualifiers, extracellular‑matrix components, immunostimulatory agents, bone‑regulating proteins, and therapeutic biomaterials interact to shape tissue ultrastructure and inform clinical strategies for bone‑related, immunologic, and metabolic conditions.

Connective‑tissue protein is the parent class of collagen, and the ReFit bone substitute is directly mapped to collagen, emphasizing its role in bone regeneration. Deficiency qualifiers are applicable to collagen, immunological adjuvant, estradiol, glucocorticoids, angiotensin amide, sex steroid hormones, sulfur and testosterone. Estradiol is a member of the “Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain” group and is used to treat menopausal disorders. Angiotensinamide is a vasoconstrictor that narrows blood vessels and modulates blood‑pressure regulation. Natriuretic peptide type B belongs to the natriuretic substance family, promotes sodium excretion and vasodilation, and counteracts fluid overload. Estrogens are employed for menopausal disorder management, providing hormonal support that alleviates vasomotor symptoms, prevents bone loss and reduces urogenital atrophy. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2079
retrieved_docs.content: Proteolytic enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as members of the kolagenasy group, indicating that they function as collagenases that degrade collagen in wound tissue and thereby facilitate debridement and healing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 631
retrieved_docs.content: Proteolytic enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are a specific class of enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers, indicating that they belong to the broader category of therapeutic wound‑debriding enzymes. They are also described as a member of the kolagenasy (collagenase) group, meaning that these proteolytic agents function as collagen‑breaking enzymes used to remove necrotic tissue and promote healing in chronic wounds and ulcers. This classification underscores their role as specialized proteolytic agents within wound‑care regimens, highlighting their importance for effective debridement and tissue repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 748
retrieved_docs.content: Hyaluronic acid is classified as a connective‑tissue protein and also functions as an immunological adjuvant; immunostimulants are a subclass of hyaluronic acid. Collagen is likewise a connective‑tissue protein. Both hyaluronic acid and collagen can be described in terms of deficiency and ultrastructure, and the qualifier “deficiency” is applicable to a broad range of entities including hyaluronic acid, collagen, the immunological adjuvant, amylases, asparaginase, estradiol, immunostimulants and prostaglandin antagonists; deficiency may specifically denote a lack of sulfur or testosterone. Amylases belong to the hydrolase enzyme family and may also be qualified by ultrastructure. The ReFit bone substitute is mapped to both hyaluronic acid and collagen, indicating its association with these structural proteins. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians and formulators in optimizing patient care, ensuring appropriate dosing, and recognizing the broader therapeutic and supportive contributions of vitamin A within diverse health‑related products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are classified as a specific type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS, indicating that they belong to the broader therapeutic category used to manage wounds and ulcers. Within this category, PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS also include cicatrizants, which are agents that promote scar formation, and enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers, highlighting the diverse mechanisms employed in wound care. Proteolytic enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are a distinct subclass of enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers; they are identified as proteolytic agents that belong to the trypsin family and are associated with kolagenasy (collagenase activity), underscoring their role in breaking down proteinaceous debris and remodeling collagen in the wound environment. This hierarchical organization links general wound‑healing preparations to specialized enzymatic agents, illustrating how specific proteolytic and collagenolytic actions are integrated into comprehensive wound‑treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 373
retrieved_docs.content: Hyaluronic acid is classified as a connective‑tissue protein and listed in the CTRP Biomarker Terminology as a clinical biomarker; it functions as both an immunostimulant and an immunological adjuvant and is mapped to the ReFit bone‑substitute product, highlighting its role in orthopedic biomaterials. Collagen, the parent protein of the connective‑tissue protein category, is directly linked to ReFit and is noted for its ultrastructural characteristics; both hyaluronic acid and collagen receive deficiency qualifiers in the biomarker system. The CTRP terminology also incorporates BCR‑ABL fusion proteins (deficiency qualifier), the adrenal steroid dehydroepiandrosterone (also an immunological adjuvant), and the broader class of sex‑steroid hormones—including estradiol, estrogeny, and testosterone—which are key regulators of reproductive development, menstrual cycles, fertility, and menopausal disorders; natural and semisynthetic esters of these hormones are used in hormone‑replacement
